headline: Malaysia Raised Its Rice Target to 80% by 2030; the Actual Rate Is 56%
context: In November 2024 the government raised its rice self-sufficiency target to 80% by 2030, up from 75%. The latest official rate is 56.2%, a gap of nearly 24 points that has not been narrowing.

cards.big: Malaysia raised its rice self-sufficiency target to 80% by 2030, up from the previous 75%.
cards.sub: The rate it is raising from sits at 56.2%, and it has not been climbing toward the goal.

cards.big: The actual self-sufficiency rate is 56.2%. The target rose to 80%; the harvest did not.
cards.sub: Closing a gap of nearly 24 points by 2030 needs the rate to climb about four points a year. It has been broadly flat.

cards.big: Malaysia buys roughly 44% of its rice abroad, tying the staple to other governments' export decisions.
cards.sub: When India, the world's largest rice exporter, curbed shipments in 2023, importing nations including Malaysia faced tighter supply and higher prices.

cards.big: The national food import bill nearly doubled in a decade, from RM42.6 billion in 2014 to RM78.8 billion in 2023.
cards.sub: That is the gross import bill, not the net food trade deficit of about RM31 billion. Both widened as the ringgit weakened.

cards.big: Raising the self-sufficiency target raises the ambition, not the harvest. The 24-point gap is closed in the paddy field, not the policy document.
Raising a self-sufficiency target without raising output is like lifting your savings goal without lifting your income.
cards.sub: The number on paper changes; the balance in the account does not.

cards.big: A government can legislate a target but not a harvest. Rice security is won in the granary, not the gazette.
